title: Lecture Notes on Replica Tensor Networks for Random Quantum Circuits
abstract: We present a pedagogical, hands-on tutorial on \emph{replica tensor-network} techniques for random quantum circuits. At its core, the method recasts circuit-averaged observables acting on multiple copies of the system as the contraction of a classical tensor network, equivalently the partition function of a statistical-mechanics model whose effective spins live in the commutant of the gate ensemble. The framework is general: changing the observable or the initial state modifies only the replica boundary conditions, while changing the ensemble modifies the bulk tensors. Focusing on quantum-information diagnostics, from metrics of wavefunction spreadings to entanglement quantifiers, we illustrate the approach in both clean and noisy random unitary circuits. We then briefly explain how the methodology extends to other ensembles, such as orthogonal or Clifford circuits. The lecture notes are accompanied by \texttt{ReplicaTN}, a self-contained C++/Python library and pedagogical notebooks.
summary: This paper presents a tutorial on Replica Tensor Networks, a method to efficiently calculate averaged quantum information metrics in random circuits. By mapping quantum circuit averages to a classical statistical mechanics model, it enables the simulation of much larger systems than standard methods. The work includes a new C++/Python library, ReplicaTN, for practical implementation.
main_problem: Developing an efficient, numerically stable framework for computing circuit-averaged non-linear quantum information metrics in random quantum circuits.
main_result: The introduction of the Replica Tensor Network (RTN) method and the ReplicaTN library, which allows for the simulation of large-scale random circuits by mapping them to a 2D classical statistical mechanics model.
method: The replica trick is used to map $k$-degree polynomial observables to a classical tensor network contraction, utilizing Weingarten calculus and a 'dressed' transfer matrix to ensure numerical stability.
model_or_system: One-dimensional brickwork random quantum circuits consisting of layers of Haar-random, orthogonal, or Clifford unitary gates acting on $N$ qudits.
key_observables: Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), Renyi entanglement entropies, Renyi-$k$ local purity, and Cross-Entropy Benchmark (XEB).
important_parameters: Replica number $k$, local dimension $d$, circuit depth $t$, system size $N$, and noise rate $p$.
main_assumptions: Gates are statistically independent and identically distributed; the method relies on the efficiency of the commutant-basis representation and SVD truncation.
figures_summary: Circuit diagrams of random brickwork; plots showing the approach of participation entropy and local purity to the Haar plateau; and the exponential decay of XEB in noisy circuits.
paper_structure: The notes provide a pedagogical tutorial starting from the formal mapping of circuit averages to classical tensor networks, moving through the mathematical machinery of Weingarten calculus, detailing the numerical implementation of the dressed transfer matrix, and concluding with applications to noise and different gate ensembles.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful tool for studying the dynamics of entanglement, scrambling, and information propagation in many-body systems and noisy quantum devices without the exponential cost of full state-vector simulation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Annealed inverse participation ratios for Haar-unitary qubit brickwork cir- cuits. (a) Annealed participation entropy eS IPR 2 (t) ≡−logE[I(2)] as a function of depth t for N ∈{32,64,128,256} (Blues palette). Dashed horizontal lines mark the Haar plateau eS IPR 2,Haar = −logI(2) Haar of Eq. (35). (b) Relative deviation from the Haar

Figure 2: Half-chain local purities on Haar-unitary qubit brickworks. (a) An- nealed local-purity entropy eS LP 2 (t) = −logE[P2(t)] as a function of depth t for N ∈{8,16,24,32} qubits and t ≤64 (Blues palette); markers are the RTN, solid lines the analytic random-walk closed form of Eq. (50). (b) The k = 3 analogue, eSLP (3)(t) = −1

Figure 3: Noisy random Haar-unitary brickwork on qubits, at every integer depth and for N ∈{8,16,32,64}. Panels (a) and (c) plot the negative log purity −logE[Tr ρ2 t ] at p = 0.10 and p = 0.20 respectively; panels (b) and (d) plot the relative entropy of coherence ˜C(2) rel (ρt) at the same two noise rates. The coherence rises linearly with depth, peaks at the sampling transition, and decays to zero as ρt →I/D becomes classical.

Figure 4: Rényi-2 coherent information I(2) c (R : B)/(K log d) of the noisy Haar- random encoder on NB qubits as a function of circuit depth t, for two values of the single-qubit depolarising strength p ∈{0.05,0.10} at fixed K = 1 reference qubit. RTN prediction at NB ∈{8,16,32}. As NB grows the curves approach a sharp cros- sover at a critical depth tc(p), the error-correction transition between the recoverable phase I(2) c →+1 and the decohered phase I(2) c →−1.

Figure 5: Linear cross-entropy benchmark χXEB(t) Eq. (67) for a Haar-random brick- work on N ∈{8,16,32,64} qubits up to depth t = 40. (a) Clean reference (no device error), saturating at the Haar fidelity plateau χXEB →(D −1)/(D + 1) →1 at depths t ≳log N. (b) Incoherent depolarising noise on the device replica, rate p = 0.10. (c) Same with p = 0.20. In both noisy panels, χXEB decays exponentially towards the classical (no-quantum-advantage) regime χXEB →0. Log-scale y-axis.
